facts.text: St. Cloud was founded on April 16, 1909 as a retirement community for Civil War Union veterans who paid $50 for five‑acre lots.
The city earned the nickname “The Friendly Soldier City” because its streets were named after the states where its founding veterans had served.
In 1923 the Veteran’s Memorial Library opened, a gift from the Woman’s Club that later became part of the Osceola County Library System.
St. Cloud’s early economy grew around a sugar factory built by Disston, whose Sugar Belt Railway linked the town to the South Florida Railroad in 1888.
The city sits on the southern shore of East Lake Tohopekaliga, about 26 miles southeast of Orlando, giving it a unique lakeside setting in Central Florida.
The historic Pioneer Village museum in St. Cloud preserves the town’s early 20th‑century life and hosts seasonal events like Pioneer Day and a spring soirée.
